content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Let's use this example to see what JavaScript actually does when it reaches an await.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The line that changes everything is this one:
await loadPreferences();
A lot of people think await simply means "wait here until the promise finishes."
That’s only half the story.
What await really does is:
- Evaluate the expression (loadPreferences()).
- Get a promise (or convert the value into one).
- Pause the current async function.
- Schedule the rest of that function as a microtask that will run once the promise settles. (The important part is this)
Think of your function as being split into two pieces.
async function fetchProfile() {
console.log("📂 Opening profile");
await loadPreferences();
console.log("✅ Profile ready");
}Conceptually, it’s similar to this:
console.log("📂 Opening profile");loadPreferences().then(() => {
console.log("✅ Profile ready");
});
That’s not exactly how JavaScript implements it internally, but it’s an excellent mental model.
The code after await behaves very much like the callback passed to .then(). It doesn't stay on the call stack waiting—it gets scheduled as a microtask.

What happens in this example?
When this line executes:
await loadPreferences();
loadPreferences() runs immediately.
async function loadPreferences() {
console.log("⚙️ Loading preferences");
}Since there’s no await inside it, it:
- logs "⚙️ Loading preferences"
- immediately returns a fulfilled promise
At this point, the promise is already resolved.
But here’s the surprising part:
Even though the promise is already fulfilled, await still pauses. It does not continue with:
console.log("✅ Profile ready");right away.
Instead, JavaScript says:
“This promise is fulfilled. Great. I’ll continue this function later as a microtask.”
So the continuation gets placed into the microtask queue. At that exact moment, the queue looks like this:
Microtask Queue
— — — — — — — — — — — — —
1. Resume fetchProfile()
Notice that "✅ Profile ready" hasn't run yet.
So JavaScript goes back to finish the rest of the script.
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("📩 Notification received");
});This adds another microtask:
Microtask Queue
— — — — — — — — — — — — —
1. Resume fetchProfile()
2. Promise.then(…)
Then the script finishes:
console.log("🏁 Initial render complete");Only now is the call stack finally empty. So JavaScript starts draining the microtask queue:
First, it resumes fetchProfile():
console.log("✅ Profile ready");Then it runs the next microtask:
Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log("📩 Notification received");
});Only after every microtask has finished does JavaScript move on to the timer.
So the final log order become :
🚀 App started
📂 Opening profile
⚙️ Loading preferences
🏁 Initial render complete
✅ Profile ready
📩 Notification received
⏰ Timer finishedUnderstanding this one behavior of await makes the event loop feel much less mysterious and helps explain why seemingly "out-of-order" logs are actually expected.
Author : Arkadi Nazarian
